content:
# Genesis Chapter 35 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 20r–20v | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_035_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_035_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Pages 32–33 | **View LXX online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0036 | https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0037

---

*God Commands Jacob: Return to Bethel (vv. 1–5)*

**35:1** And God said [וַיֹּאמֶר אֱלֹהִים] to Jacob [אֶל-יַעֲקֹב] — Arise [קוּם] and go up [וַעֲלֵה] to Bethel [בֵּית-אֵל] and dwell there [וְשֶׁב-שָׁם] — and make there [וַעֲשֵׂה-שָׁם] an altar [מִזְבֵּחַ] to God [לָאֵל] who appeared [הַנִּרְאֶה] to you [אֵלֶיךָ] in your fleeing [בְּבָרְחֲךָ] from the face of [מִפְּנֵי] Esau [עֵשָׂו] your brother [אָחִיךָ].

**35:2** And Jacob said [וַיֹּאמֶר יַעֲקֹב] to his household [אֶל-בֵּיתוֹ] and to all [וְאֶל-כָּל] who were with him [אֲשֶׁר עִמּוֹ] — Put away [הָסִרוּ] the foreign gods [אֶת-אֱלֹהֵי הַנֵּכָר] that are in your midst [אֲשֶׁר בְּתֹכְכֶם] — and purify yourselves [וְהִטַּהֲרוּ] — and change [וְהַחֲלִיפוּ] your garments [שִׂמְלֹתֵיכֶם].

**35:3** And let us arise [וְנָקוּמָה] and go up [וְנַעֲלֶה] to Bethel [בֵּית-אֵל] — and I will make there [וְאֶעֱשֶׂה-שָׁם] an altar [מִזְבֵּחַ] to God [לָאֵל] who answered me [הָעֹנֶה אֹתִי] in the day [בְּיוֹם] of my distress [צָרָתִי] — and was [וַיְהִי] with me [עִמָּדִי] in the way [בַּדֶּרֶךְ] that I went [אֲשֶׁר הָלַכְתִּי].

**35:4** And they gave [וַיִּתְּנוּ] to Jacob [אֶל-יַעֲקֹב] all the foreign gods [אֵת כָּל-אֱלֹהֵי הַנֵּכָר] that were in their hands [אֲשֶׁר בְּיָדָם] — and the rings [וְאֶת-הַנְּזָמִים] that were in their ears [אֲשֶׁר בְּאָזְנֵיהֶם] — and Jacob buried them [וַיִּטְמֹן אֹתָם יַעֲקֹב] under the terebinth [תַּחַת הָאֵלָה] that was by [אֲשֶׁר עִם] Shechem [שְׁכֶם].

**35:5** And they journeyed [וַיִּסְעוּ] — and a terror of God [וַיְהִי חִתַּת אֱלֹהִים] was [וַיְהִי] upon the cities [עַל-הֶעָרִים] that were around them [אֲשֶׁר סְבִיבוֹתֵיהֶם] — and they did not pursue [וְלֹא רָדְפוּ] after the sons of Jacob [אַחֲרֵי בְּנֵי יַעֲקֹב].

---

*Arrival at Bethel — El-Bethel — Deborah's Death (vv. 6–8)*

**35:6** And Jacob came [וַיָּבֹא יַעֲקֹב] — he and all the people [הוּא וְכָל-הָעָם] that were with him [אֲשֶׁר עִמּוֹ] — to Luz [לוּזָה] which is in [אֲשֶׁר בְּ] the land of Canaan [אֶרֶץ כְּנָעַן] — that is [הִוא] Bethel [בֵּית-אֵל] — he and all the people [הוּא וְכָל-הָעָם] that were with him [אֲשֶׁר עִמּוֹ].

**35:7** And he built there [וַיִּבֶן שָׁם] an altar [מִזְבֵּחַ] and he called [וַיִּקְרָא] the place [לַמָּקוֹם] El-Bethel [אֵל בֵּית-אֵל] — because [כִּי] there [שָׁם] God revealed himself [נִגְלוּ אֵלָיו הָאֱלֹהִים] to him [אֵלָיו] in his fleeing [בְּבָרְחוֹ] from the face of [מִפְּנֵי] his brother [אָחִיו].

**35:8** And Deborah [וַתָּמָת דְּבֹרָה] — the nurse of Rebekah [מֵינֶקֶת רִבְקָה] — died [וַתָּמָת] — and she was buried [וַתִּקָּבֵר] below Bethel [מִתַּחַת לְבֵית-אֵל] under the oak [תַּחַת הָאַלּוֹן] — and he called [וַיִּקְרָא] its name [שְׁמוֹ] Allon-bacuth [אַלּוֹן בָּכוּת] [that is: *Oak of Weeping*].

---

*God Appears — El Shaddai — The Israel Naming Confirmed (vv. 9–13)*

**35:9** And God appeared [וַיֵּרָא אֱלֹהִים] to Jacob [אֶל-יַעֲקֹב] again [עוֹד] in his coming [בְּבֹאוֹ] from Paddan-aram [מִפַּדַּן אֲרָם] — and he blessed him [וַיְבָרֶךְ אֹתוֹ].

**35:10** And God said [וַיֹּאמֶר-לוֹ אֱלֹהִים] to him — Your name [שִׁמְךָ] is Jacob [יַעֲקֹב] — your name [שִׁמְךָ] shall not be called [לֹא-יִקָּרֵא] Jacob [יַעֲקֹב] anymore [עוֹד] — but [כִּי] Israel [יִשְׂרָאֵל] shall be your name [יִהְיֶה שְׁמֶךָ] — and he called [וַיִּקְרָא] his name [אֶת-שְׁמוֹ] Israel [יִשְׂרָאֵל].

**35:11** And God said [וַיֹּאמֶר לוֹ אֱלֹהִים] to him — I am El Shaddai [אֲנִי אֵל שַׁדַּי] — be fruitful [פְּרֵה] and multiply [וּרְבֵה] — a nation [גּוֹי] and a company of nations [וּקְהַל גּוֹיִם] shall be from you [יִהְיֶה מִמֶּךָּ] — and kings [וּמְלָכִים] shall come forth [יֵצְאוּ] from your loins [מֵחֲלָצֶיךָ].

**35:12** And the land [וְאֶת-הָאָרֶץ] that I gave [אֲשֶׁר נָתַתִּי] to Abraham [לְאַבְרָהָם] and to Isaac [וּלְיִצְחָק] — to you [לְךָ] I will give it [אֶתְּנֶנָּה] — and to your seed [וּלְזַרְעֲךָ] after you [אַחֲרֶיךָ] I will give [אֶתֵּן] the land [אֶת-הָאָרֶץ].

**35:13** And God went up [וַיַּעַל אֱלֹהִים] from upon him [מֵעָלָיו] in the place [בַּמָּקוֹם] where he had spoken with him [אֲשֶׁר דִּבֶּר אִתּוֹ].

---

*Jacob's Massebah at Bethel (vv. 14–15)*

**35:14** And Jacob set up [וַיַּצֵּב יַעֲקֹב] a standing-pillar [מַצֵּבָה] in the place [בַּמָּקוֹם] where he had spoken with him [אֲשֶׁר דִּבֶּר אִתּוֹ] — a pillar of stone [מַצֶּבֶת אָבֶן] — and he poured [וַיַּסֵּךְ] upon it [עָלֶיהָ] a drink-offering [נֶסֶךְ] — and he poured [וַיִּצֹק] upon it [עָלֶיהָ] oil [שֶׁמֶן].

**35:15** And Jacob called [וַיִּקְרָא יַעֲקֹב] the name of [אֶת-שֵׁם] the place [הַמָּקוֹם] where God had spoken with him [אֲשֶׁר דִּבֶּר אִתּוֹ שָׁם אֱלֹהִים] — Bethel [בֵּית-אֵל].

---

*Journey from Bethel — Rachel's Labor and Death (vv. 16–20)*

**35:16** And they journeyed [וַיִּסְעוּ] from Bethel [מִבֵּית אֵל] — and there was [וַיְהִי] still [עוֹד] a kivrat-ha'arets [כִּבְרַת-הָאָרֶץ] [*a stretch of land — a measure of distance, exact quantity uncertain*] to come [לָבוֹא] to Ephrath [אֶפְרָתָה] — and Rachel went into labor [וַתֵּלֶד רָחֵל] — and she had [וַיְהִי] hard [לְהַקְשׁוֹת] labor [בְּלִדְתָּהּ].

**35:17** And it came to pass [וַיְהִי] in her laboring hard [בְּהַקְשֹׁתָהּ בְּלִדְתָּהּ] that the midwife [וַתֹּאמֶר לָהּ הַמְיַלֶּדֶת] said to her — Do not fear [אַל-תִּירְאִי] — for also this one [כִּי-גַם-זֶה] is a son for you [לָךְ בֵּן].

**35:18** And it came to pass [וַיְהִי] in the departing [בְּצֵאת] of her soul [נַפְשָׁהּ] — for [כִּי] she died [מֵתָה] — that she called [וַתִּקְרָא] his name [שְׁמוֹ] Ben-oni [בֶּן-אוֹנִי] [*son of my sorrow / son of my strength*] — but his father [וְאָבִיו] called him [קָרָא-לוֹ] Benjamin [בִּנְיָמִין] [*son of the right hand / son of the south*].

**35:19** And Rachel died [וַתָּמָת רָחֵל] — and she was buried [וַתִּקָּבֵר] on the way [בְּדֶרֶךְ] to Ephrath [אֶפְרָתָה] — that is [הִוא] Bethlehem [בֵּית לָחֶם].

**35:20** And Jacob set up [וַיַּצֵּב יַעֲקֹב] a pillar [מַצֵּבָה] over her grave [עַל-קְבֻרָתָהּ] — that is [הִוא] the pillar of [מַצֵּבַת] the grave of Rachel [קְבֻרַת-רָחֵל] until this day [עַד הַיּוֹם].

---

*Israel Journeys — Reuben's Sin (vv. 21–22a)*

**35:21** And Israel journeyed [וַיִּסַּע יִשְׂרָאֵל] — and he spread [וַיֵּט] his tent [אָהֳלֹה] beyond [מֵהָלְאָה לְ] the tower of Eder [מִגְדַּל-עֵדֶר].

**35:22** And it came to pass [וַיְהִי] in Israel's dwelling [בִּשְׁכֹּן יִשְׂרָאֵל] in that land [בָּאָרֶץ הַהִוא] — that Reuben went [וַיֵּלֶךְ רְאוּבֵן] and lay with [וַיִּשְׁכַּב אֶת] Bilhah [בִּלְהָה] — the concubine of his father [פִּילֶגֶשׁ אָבִיו] — and Israel heard [וַיִּשְׁמַע יִשְׂרָאֵל].

---

*The Twelve Sons of Jacob (vv. 22b–26)*

And the sons of Jacob [וַיִּהְיוּ בְנֵי-יַעֲקֹב] were twelve [שְׁנֵים עָשָׂר]:

**35:23** The sons of Leah [בְּנֵי לֵאָה] — the firstborn of Jacob [בְּכוֹר יַעֲקֹב] — Reuben [רְאוּבֵן] — and Simeon [וְשִׁמְעוֹן] and Levi [וְלֵוִי] and Judah [וִיהוּדָה] and Issachar [וְיִשָּׂשכָר] and Zebulun [וּזְבוּלֻן].

**35:24** The sons of Rachel [בְּנֵי רָחֵל] — Joseph [יוֹסֵף] and Benjamin [וּבִנְיָמִן].

**35:25** And the sons of Bilhah [וּבְנֵי בִלְהָה] — the handmaid of Rachel [שִׁפְחַת רָחֵל] — Dan [דָּן] and Naphtali [וְנַפְתָּלִי].

**35:26** And the sons of Zilpah [וּבְנֵי זִלְפָּה] — the handmaid of Leah [שִׁפְחַת לֵאָה] — Gad [גָּד] and Asher [וְאָשֵׁר] — these are the sons of Jacob [אֵלֶּה בְּנֵי יַעֲקֹב] who were born to him [אֲשֶׁר יֻלַּד-לוֹ] in Paddan-aram [בְּפַדַּן אֲרָם].

---

*Jacob Comes to Isaac — Isaac's Death (vv. 27–29)*

**35:27** And Jacob came [וַיָּבֹא יַעֲקֹב] to Isaac [אֶל-יִצְחָק] his father [אָבִיו] — to Mamre [מַמְרֵא] — Kiriath-arba [קִרְיַת הָאַרְבַּע] — that is [הִוא] Hebron [חֶבְרוֹן] — where [אֲשֶׁר] Abraham [אַבְרָהָם] and Isaac [וְיִצְחָק] had sojourned [גָּרוּ].

**35:28** And the days of Isaac [וַיִּהְיוּ יְמֵי יִצְחָק] were [וַיִּהְיוּ] a hundred [מְאַת] and eighty [שְׁמֹנִים] years [שָׁנָה].

**35:29** And Isaac breathed his last [וַיִּגְוַע יִצְחָק] and he died [וַיָּמָת] and was gathered [וַיֵּאָסֶף] to his peoples [אֶל-עַמָּיו] — old [זָקֵן] and full of days [וּשְׂבַע יָמִים] — and Esau [וַיִּקְבְּרוּ אֹתוֹ עֵשָׂו] and Jacob [וְיַעֲקֹב] buried him [בָּנָיו].

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

**On reading the Leningrad Codex, Folios 20r–20v:**

Folio 20r (GEN_035_page_001.jpg) is the recto of the twentieth leaf of the Leningrad Codex Genesis section, photographed in color by the West Semitic Research Project. The folio marker כ (twenty) is visible in the upper corner. The three-column layout in Tiberian-vocalized Sephardic square script is clearly legible throughout, though the left margin of the leftmost column is slightly worn. The folio opens with the conclusion of Genesis 34 — Jacob's rebuke of Simeon and Levi — in its final verses in the left column, and then the middle and right columns carry Genesis 35:1 through approximately verse 18, through Rachel's death in labor. The section marking for the new pericope beginning at 35:1 is visible. Full cantillation marks (te'amim) are present throughout; vocalization is complete and consistent with the Tiberian tradition.

Folio 20v (GEN_035_page_002.jpg) carries Genesis 35 from approximately verse 19 through verse 29 in its opening columns, followed by the beginning of Genesis 36 (Esau's genealogy). The folio marker כ״א (twenty-one) is visible. The text from Rachel's burial through the twelve-sons list, Jacob's arrival at Isaac, and Isaac's death and burial is fully legible. A marginal masorah notation is visible at verse 22 — adjacent to the Reuben incident — which in the traditional Masoretic text marks the significant *setumah* (closed section break) that falls after "and Israel heard." The scribe's hand is consistent and clear; no lacunae are present.

**On reading Codex Vaticanus, Pages 32–33:**

Page 32 (GEN_035_vaticanus_page_001.jpg) bears the chapter heading **Γεν XXXIX** at the top center, which is the LXX chapter enumeration for the material corresponding to MT Genesis 34's conclusion and the opening of Genesis 35. The continuous three-column Greek uncial text, without word-breaks, is legible throughout. This page carries the end of the Genesis 34 narrative and Genesis 35:1 through approximately verse 15.

Page 33 (GEN_035_vaticanus_page_002.jpg) bears the heading **Γεν XL** at top center, carrying the remainder of Genesis 35 through the beginning of Genesis 36 genealogy. Rachel's death, the twelve-sons roster, and Isaac's death are all present on this page in the right-hand and middle columns.

---

**35:1 — *beborachakha mippenei Esav achikha* (בְּבָרְחֲךָ מִפְּנֵי עֵשָׂו אָחִיךָ) — God's Reminder: The Flight from Esau:**

The divine command to return to Bethel is anchored in a specific biographical memory: the God who appeared to Jacob at Bethel during his flight from Esau (Genesis 28:10–22) is now recalling that original encounter. The infinitive construct בְּבָרְחֲךָ (*beborachakha*, "in your fleeing") combined with מִפְּנֵי (*mippenei*, "from the face of") is precise — Jacob did not merely "leave" Esau's territory; he fled in fear from Esau's face. God identifies himself by that moment of vulnerability. This is the same Bethel where Jacob saw the stairway (*sullam*) and received the first covenant promise; the return completes a structural arc in the Jacob narrative.

The LXX reads *en tōi apodidraskonti se apo prosōpou Esau tou adelphou sou* — "in your fleeing from the face of Esau your brother" — exact rendering of both the infinitival construction and *mippenei*.

---

**35:2–4 — *elohei hannekhar* (אֱלֹהֵי הַנֵּכָר) and the Earrings: Purification Before Bethel:**

The command to "put away the foreign gods" (הָסִרוּ אֶת-אֱלֹהֵי הַנֵּכָר, *hasiru et-elohei hannekhar*) is the direct consequence of the Shechem sojourn — and implicitly of the Laban household, where Rachel had stolen Laban's *teraphim* (Genesis 31:19, 34–35). The household of Jacob contains cultic objects that are incompatible with a journey to the altar of El. The purification command has two components: (1) put away the foreign gods (*hasiru*) — the Hiphil of סוּר (*sur*, "to remove, to turn away, to depart"), indicating their removal from the person and from the camp; and (2) change your garments (*hachalifu simlotheikhem*) — ritual purity expressed in the donning of clean clothes, parallel to the priestly purification rites later systematized in Leviticus.

The earrings (*nozamim*, הַנְּזָמִים) of verse 4 are identified alongside the foreign gods as items to be relinquished. The *nezem* (singular: נֶזֶם, "ring, earring, nose-ring") throughout the ancient Near East functioned as both jewelry and amulet — protective talismans with cultic associations. The combination of "foreign gods" *and* "earrings" in a single surrender ceremony confirms that the jewelry was understood as pagan amulet-objects, not merely ornamental.

Jacob buries them all *tachat ha'elah asher im Shekhem* — "under the terebinth that was by Shechem." The choice of Shechem as the burial site is pointed: the objects tainted by the Shechem affair (and by Laban's household) are buried in the soil of the city that was their proximate occasion.

The LXX renders *elohei hannekhar* as *tous theous tous allotriou* (τοὺς θεοὺς τοὺς ἀλλοτρίους, "the foreign gods") — exact. For the earrings the LXX reads *ta enōtia* (τὰ ἐνώτια, "the ear-rings"), consistent with MT *nozamim*.

---

**35:5 — *chittat Elohim* (חִתַּת אֱלֹהִים) — "A Terror of God": Divine Protection on the Road:**

The noun חִתַּת (*chittat*, construct form of חִתָּה, "terror, dread") combined with אֱלֹהִים (*Elohim*, "God") forms a genitive of source or agency: "a terror whose source was God" / "a God-sent terror." The idiom recurs in Deuteronomy 2:25 and 11:25 — God tells Israel that he will put *pachad* and *chittah* ("dread and terror") upon the nations so they will not attack. Here the divine protection takes the form not of military force but of supernatural dread paralyzing the surrounding Canaanite cities: they had every motivation to avenge the Shechem massacre, but divine terror held them immobile.

The LXX reads *ekstasis theou* (ἔκστασις θεοῦ, "an astonishment/ecstasy of God") — *ekstasis* rendering the more general sense of the noun, covering both terror and stupefaction. The MT *chittat* is more specifically "dread" while *ekstasis* broadens to "trance-like bewilderment."

---

**35:7 — *niggelu elav ha'Elohim* (נִגְלוּ אֵלָיו הָאֱלֹהִים) — "God [plural verb] Revealed Himself to Him": A Striking Grammatical Construction:**

The verb נִגְלוּ (*niggelu*) is the Niphal perfect 3rd person *plural* of גָּלָה (*galah*, "to reveal, to uncover, to go into exile"), yet the subject is הָאֱלֹהִים (*ha'Elohim*, "God") which, though formally plural in morphology, ordinarily takes singular verbs when used as a name for the one God (cf. Genesis 1:1 where *bara'* is 3rd person singular with *Elohim*). Here the plural verb *niggelu* ("they/he revealed themselves/himself") is used with *Elohim* — a construction that is grammatically anomalous by the usual rules, and which ancient, medieval, and modern commentators have noted. The Talmudic and rabbinic tradition (e.g., *Berakhot* 13a; Rashi on this verse) read this as implying the plurality of the theophany — the presence of God's *mal'akhim* (messengers/angels) as well as the divine presence itself was revealed to Jacob at Bethel. Some critical scholars take the plural as a trace of an older polytheistic or henotheistic usage preserved in the text. EMET translates the plural verb literally — "revealed himself" is an interpretive smoothing; "God revealed [themselves]" — and preserves the anomaly in a note.

The LXX reads *ōphthē autōi ho theos* (ὤφθη αὐτῷ ὁ θεός, "God appeared to him") — singular verb *ōphthē*, regularizing the MT's grammatical anomaly. This is a significant LXX departure from the MT syntax.

---

**35:8 — *Devorah meineqet Rivqah* (דְּבֹרָה מֵינֶקֶת רִבְקָה) — Deborah the Nurse of Rebekah: The Unexpected Death:**

The death of Deborah is one of the most cryptic and poignant moments in Genesis. She is identified as מֵינֶקֶת רִבְקָה (*meineqet Rivqah*, "the nurse of Rebekah") — a connection that reaches back to Genesis 24:59, where "Rebekah and her nurse" left with Abraham's servant. That nurse is not named in Genesis 24. Here she is finally named, only at her death: **דְּבֹרָה** (*Devorah*, "Bee" — the same name as the later prophetess/judge of Judges 4–5). She has traveled with Rebekah from Mesopotamia to Canaan, lived through the births of Esau and Jacob, presumably accompanied Rebekah's household — and somehow came to be with Jacob's household now, in his old age. Rebekah herself has never received a death notice in Genesis (the text never records her death, only Isaac's death in this chapter). Deborah is buried in Rebekah's stead.

The place-name **אַלּוֹן בָּכוּת** (*Allon Bakhut*, "Oak of Weeping") encodes the grief: the oak by Bethel became named for the tears shed at Deborah's grave. The name *bacuth* (בָּכוּת) is the noun form of בָּכָה (*bakah*, "to weep") — "weeping" as an abstract noun.

The LXX renders the site as *Bathos penthous* (Βάθος πένθους, "Depth of Mourning") — not "Oak of Weeping" but "Depth/Hollow of Mourning," a significant interpretive departure. LXX may have read a different Vorlage or have been translating thematically.

---

**35:9–13 — The Second Israel-Naming: Relation to Genesis 32:28:**

The renaming of Jacob as Israel here (v.10) is the second account of this naming in Genesis — the first occurring at Peniel (Genesis 32:28–30) after Jacob's wrestling with the mysterious *ish* ("man"). Several features of the relationship between the two accounts require notice:

1. **At Peniel (32:28):** The renaming is performed by the wrestler (whom many traditions identify as an angel or a divine being), in the context of a physical struggle. The name is given with an etymology: *ki sarita im Elohim ve'im anashim vatukhal* ("for you have striven with God and with men, and you have prevailed").

2. **Here at Bethel (35:10):** God (Elohim) himself performs and confirms the renaming, with the formula *lo-yiqareh shimkha ya'aqov od ki Yisra'el yihyeh shemekha* ("your name shall not be called Jacob anymore, but Israel shall be your name"). No etymology is given here; the act of renaming is purely confirmatory and declaratory.

The two accounts have generated substantial discussion in historical-critical scholarship regarding the documentary hypothesis (J and E/P sources), but EMET observes only the literary datum: the Bethel account formally institutionalizes and confirms what Peniel initiated. The continued use of both "Jacob" and "Israel" interchangeably throughout the subsequent narrative (e.g., v. 21 "Israel journeyed," v. 22 "Reuben lay with Bilhah... and Israel heard") suggests the narrator does not treat the name-change as obliterating the old name but as adding a new dimension to the patriarch's identity.

---

**35:11 — *El Shaddai* (אֵל שַׁדַּי) — Untranslated Divine Name:**

Per project convention, *El Shaddai* is preserved untranslated. The divine name אֵל שַׁדַּי appears here as the name God uses for self-identification, previously used at the Abrahamic covenant of circumcision (Genesis 17:1) and in Isaac's blessing of Jacob (28:3). Its appearance here connects the Bethel theophany to the larger patriarchal covenant chain. The etymology of *Shaddai* remains disputed: traditional renderings include "the Almighty" (from *shadad*, "to overpower"), "the Mountain-God" (from Akkadian *shadu*, "mountain"), and others. EMET declines to render what remains etymologically unresolved.

The blessing formula given here — *peru urevu* (פְּרוּ וּרְבוּ, "be fruitful and multiply") — echoes the creation mandate of Genesis 1:28 and the Noahic covenant of 9:1. The promise **גּוֹי וּקְהַל גּוֹיִם** (*goy uqehal goyim*, "a nation and a company of nations") extends beyond Abraham's promise of a single nation to an assembly of nations. The term **קְהַל** (*qahal*, "assembly, congregation, company") will later become the standard term for the assembled community of Israel (*qehal Yisra'el*).

The LXX reads *ego eimi ho theos sou* (ἐγώ εἰμι ὁ θεός σου, "I am your God") without rendering *Shaddai* — it simply does not appear in the Greek at this point, or it is subsumed into the broader *ho theos* formulation. This is a notable LXX omission/simplification.

---

**35:14 — *nesekh* and *shemen* (נֶסֶךְ / שֶׁמֶן) — Drink-Offering and Oil on the Massebah:**

The *massebah* (מַצֵּבָה, "standing pillar, sacred standing-stone") erected at Bethel is the same *massebah* that Jacob first set up in Genesis 28:18, where he poured oil upon it after his initial dream-vision. Here the rite is expanded: a *nesekh* (נֶסֶךְ, "drink-offering, libation") — poured (*vayyassekh*, Hiphil of נָסַךְ, "to pour out a libation") — and then oil (*shemen*) poured upon it (*vayyitssoq*, Qal of יָצַק, "to pour, to cast"). The libation is the earlier ritual act; the oil-anointing recalls the original Bethel act of 28:18. The *massebah* is thus consecrated by the oldest form of Israelite sacred dedication: not an altar for sacrifice but a standing stone sanctified by libation and anointing — a form of ritual that later Deuteronomic legislation will prohibit (Deuteronomy 16:22: "you shall not set up a *massebah*, which the LORD your God hates") but which is here presented as patriarchal devotion without any editorial condemnation.

---

**35:16 — *kivrat-ha'arets* (כִּבְרַת-הָאָרֶץ) — Distance Measure of Unknown Quantity:**

The term **כִּבְרַת הָאָרֶץ** (*kivrat ha'arets*, literally "a *kivrah* of the land") appears in the Hebrew Bible only in Genesis 35:16, 48:7, and 2 Kings 5:19. It is a measure of distance — the land-equivalent of some spatial unit — but its precise value is entirely unknown. The word **כִּבְרָה** (*kivrah*) has no established etymology and no cognate in other Semitic languages that clarifies its quantity. Ancient versions are inconsistent: the Septuagint sometimes renders it with *hippodromos* (a "horse-track" — approximately one stadium, perhaps 200 meters) or with *brachou chorion* ("a small distance/space"). The Targums render it variably. EMET transliterates and brackets an explanatory note: this is a distance unit, not a word for which English has an equivalent. The narrative context suggests it was "some distance — not far" from Ephrath, given the urgency of Rachel's labor.

---

**35:18 — *Ben-oni* (בֶּן-אוֹנִי) and *Binyamin* (בִּנְיָמִין) — The Dying Mother's Name and the Father's Renaming:**

**בֶּן-אוֹנִי** (*Ben-oni*): The word אוֹן (*on*, or in construct: אוֹנִי, *oni*) is polysemous in biblical Hebrew — it can mean:
  - *sorrow, grief, trouble* (from אָנַן / אָנָה, "to mourn, to grieve") — in this context the most likely sense for the dying Rachel naming her child from her death-suffering
  - *vigor, strength, generative power* (as in Job 18:12, 40:16; Deuteronomy 21:17 — "the firstfruits of my strength/vigor" — same word) — giving the name an alternative heroic reading: "son of my strength/power"

The ambiguity is irresolvable from the word alone; the death context strongly favors "son of my sorrow," but the Hebrew word genuinely holds both senses simultaneously.

**בִּנְיָמִין** (*Binyamin*): Etymologically transparent: בֵּן (*ben*, "son") + יָמִין (*yamin*, "right hand / south"). The "right hand" (*yamin*) is the hand of power, favor, and blessing in Hebrew idiom (Psalm 16:11, 110:1, etc.). The south (*yamin* also meaning south, since south is to the right when one faces east) connects Benjamin's territory in later Israel to the region south of Ephraim. Jacob renames the child "son of the right hand" — the son of favor and strength — replacing the mother's dying lament with a father's proclamation of life and status. The literary contrast is stark: Rachel names from death, Jacob names toward life.

The LXX reads *Uie odunes mou* (Υἱέ ὀδύνης μου, "Son of my pain") for Ben-oni — taking the sorrow etymology. For Benjamin it reads *Beniamin* (Βενιαμίν) — transliteration only.

---

**35:22 — *vayyishma' Yisra'el* (וַיִּשְׁמַע יִשְׂרָאֵל) — "And Israel Heard": The Most Significant Silence in Genesis:**

The Reuben-Bilhah incident is narrated in a single clause — with devastating economy. What follows "and Israel heard" is *nothing*. The Masoretic text marks a **setumah** (סְתוּמָה — a "closed" paragraph break, indicated in the Leningrad Codex by a space within the line) *after* "and Israel heard" and *before* the twelve-sons enumeration. This paragraph break is visible in the Leningrad Codex manuscript (folio 20v) precisely at this juncture. The setumah signals an abrupt ending to the incident — the narrative closes before Jacob's response is ever given.

The silence of *vayyishma' Yisra'el* is one of the most studied syntactic lacunae in the entire Pentateuch. What did Israel do? Nothing is said. Later — in Genesis 49:3–4 — Jacob's final blessing of Reuben will invoke this incident obliquely: "you went up to your father's bed; then you defiled [it] — he went up to my couch." But here, in the moment, there is only: *Israel heard.* Period. New paragraph.

Bilhah is Rachel's handmaid (35:25), the mother of Dan and Naphtali. Reuben's act with his father's concubine is a claim to patriarchal succession — the same logic by which Absalom will lie with David's concubines (2 Samuel 16:22) and Adonijah's request for Abishag will be a claim to David's throne (1 Kings 2:22). Reuben's act asserts supremacy over his father's household. It will cost him the firstborn blessing.

EMET renders the clause and its silence as they stand, without interpretive addition.

---

**35:26 — *yullad-lo be-Paddan Aram* (אֲשֶׁר יֻלַּד-לוֹ בְּפַדַּן אֲרָם) — "Who Were Born to Him in Paddan-aram":**

The roster notes that these sons were born to Jacob *in Paddan-aram* — which is technically inaccurate for Benjamin, who was just born *on the road to Ephrath* (vv. 16–18) within the land of Canaan. Benjamin is included in the general formula here without caveat. Rashi and Ibn Ezra both note this difficulty; the traditional explanation is that the list formula uses the *majority* location (all the other eleven were indeed born in Paddan-aram) as the summary description, or that Benjamin's birth during travel is subsumed under the "Paddan-aram era." EMET renders the formula as written, noting the tension.

---

**35:29 — *vayye'asef el-ammav* (וַיֵּאָסֶף אֶל-עַמָּיו) — "And He Was Gathered to His Peoples":**

The formulaic death notice **וַיֵּאָסֶף אֶל-עַמָּיו** (*vayye'asef el-ammav*, "and he was gathered to his peoples") is the standard patriarchal death formula in Genesis, appearing also for Abraham (Genesis 25:8), Ishmael (25:17), and later for Jacob himself (49:33). The verb אָסַף (*asaph*, "to gather, to collect, to bring in") in the Niphal — *vayye'asef* — is passive: he was gathered, received, taken in. The destination is *ammav* — "his peoples," a plural: the community of ancestors, the gathered dead, the family of those who went before. The formula implies continuity of existence after death — a gathering to a company, not annihilation — while remaining deliberately non-specific about the mechanics of that continuity.

Isaac dies at **180 years** (*me'at ushemonim shanah*), the same number as an Abrahamic reference point: Abraham died at 175 (Genesis 25:7), Jacob will die at 147 (47:28). The patriarchal ages form a declining sequence in the narrative arc.

**זָקֵן וּשְׂבַע יָמִים** (*zaqen usheva yamim*, "old and full of days") — the same phrase used of Abraham at his death (Genesis 25:8). *Sava' yamim* ("satisfied/sated with days") is the Hebrew idiom for a life fully lived — not merely long but complete. EMET renders it literally: "full of days."

The LXX reads *kai prosetethē pros to genos autou* (καὶ προσετέθη πρὸς τὸ γένος αὐτοῦ, "and he was added to his kin/race") — using *prostithemai* ("to be added to") for the MT's *asaph* ("to be gathered"), and *genos* ("kin, race, family") for *ammav* ("his peoples"). Semantically equivalent.

---

**LXX Vaticanus Observations (Pages 32–33):**

Codex Vaticanus pages 32–33 carry Genesis 35 in continuous Greek uncial script in three columns per page, without word-breaks or accent marks. Page 32 bears the header **Γεν XXXIX** (corresponding to the LXX chapter numbering of Genesis 34's conclusion and the opening of Genesis 35). Page 33 bears the header **Γεν XL**, indicating the LXX chapter division point that falls within the MT's Genesis 35 material.

Key LXX variants and observations for Genesis 35:

1. **35:1 — *en tōi apodidraskonti se*** — LXX preserves the infinitive construction of MT *beborachakha* with the participle *apodidraskonti* ("fleeing away"), adding *se* ("you") explicitly. Exact.

2. **35:5 — *ekstasis theou*** — LXX renders MT *chittat Elohim* ("terror of God") as *ekstasis theou* ("astonishment/stupor of God"). *Ekstasis* is broader than MT *chittat* ("dread/terror") — it encompasses awe-induced immobility.

3. **35:7 — *ōphthē autōi ho theos* (singular verb)** — The LXX regularizes the MT's anomalous plural *niggelu* ("they/he revealed") with the singular *ōphthē* ("appeared"). Significant grammatical smoothing.

4. **35:8 — *Bathos penthous* vs. MT *Allon Bakhut*** — LXX reads "Depth/Hollow of Mourning" rather than "Oak of Weeping." *Bathos* ("depth") rather than *allon* ("oak") suggests either a different Vorlage, an interpretive reading of the root, or a translator's expansive rendering of the mourning concept. The MT's *allon* (אַלּוֹן, "oak") is not obviously translatable as "depth," making a different Vorlage possible.

5. **35:11 — *El Shaddai* absent from LXX** — The LXX at this verse reads *ego eimi ho theos sou* without any equivalent of *Shaddai*. The divine name *El Shaddai* is either not in the LXX Vorlage here or is subsumed into the general divine title. Compare Genesis 17:1 where the LXX does render *El Shaddai* as *ho theos sou* similarly. The LXX systematically avoids *Shaddai* as a distinct name.

6. **35:16 — *hippodromos*** — LXX at 35:16 renders *kivrat ha'arets* as *hippodromos* (a race-course, approximately one stadium in length) — a precise but uncertain approximation. The LXX translator evidently had a traditional gloss for this measure, though the original quantity remains unknown.

7. **35:18 — *Uie odunes mou*** — LXX renders *Ben-oni* interpretively as "Son of my pain" (*odune* = pain, distress), taking the grief etymology of *on*/*oni*. This forecloses the alternative "son of my strength" reading.

8. **35:22 — *kai ēkousen Israēl*** — LXX renders *vayyishma' Yisra'el* as *kai ēkousen Israēl* ("and Israel heard") — preserving the same syntactic break and silence as the MT. The *setumah* pause of the MT is represented in the LXX by the same abrupt ending before the twelve-sons list.

9. **35:29 — *prostetethē pros to genos autou*** — "was added to his kin/race" for MT *vayye'asef el-ammav* ("gathered to his peoples"). Semantically close.

**DSS Note:**
Per the chapter manifest, no significant Genesis 35 fragments from the Dead Sea Scrolls are extant. The Leningrad Codex (primary Hebrew witness, Folios 20r–20v) and Codex Vaticanus LXX (secondary Greek witness, Pages 32–33) are the two principal manuscript sources examined for this chapter.
